speaker: interviewer
text: You're in New Haven now for 30-some-odd years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have your business. What about family-- children and grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two beautiful daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jeannie, and Cindy. I have the two older grandchildren-- Seth, boy. There's Alyson, beautiful girl. Then we have the younger ones. It's Jamie, who's 10, Stacy, who is six and a half. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, when you think back over everything you've experienced in your life, what do you think that your experience with the Holocaust [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] has meant to you, in terms of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: It meant a lot. The only sad part is, now especially, in the last year, we lost a lot of friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Meaning that they died?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a very sad part of it. And we're afraid [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in another few years, there's hardly anybody gonna be left to talk about it, to tell about it. So the world should never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often do you think about what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time. Never, never forget it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of nights I don't sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens? What do you dream about?

speaker: interviewee
text: About everything. How they took me away. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How I never saw my parents nor anybody after that. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you think what's going to be after we are gone? Is anybody gonna care? Is anybody gonna talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're afraid, to dwell even now, some of them are-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that, you know, a lot of people that-- that think that it never happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We are still the live proof now, whoever is left. I go around into schools, talk about it to the kids. I get the replies from children. I can see some children crying while I'm-- while I am talking about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But you know, it's 50 years-- over 50 years since we were liberated. Everybody is getting older. And we're just afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of feeling, even though it's been over 50 years, what kind of feeling has it left you with today about Germans and about Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: The same feeling like it happened. To me, it's like it happened yesterday. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can remember some things like it happened yesterday, not 50 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you were to look at the experience and think about what lesson it taught you about yourself, what would that be? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what you mean by that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were able to survive and sustain everything that you did. What has that meant to you about yourself, and how you think about yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: That I am one of the lucky ones that I made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you ever think about why you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after I got married, I got, thank God, a beautiful family. And I want to be around to see them, enjoy them, and to see everything good happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, one last question for you, Mr. Glucksman. As you know, a lot of people will have a chance, over time, to see our interview together today. And if you could leave one message for what you would want people to walk away with having heard your story, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just whoever don't know anything about it, they shouldn't forget it. But they already see it's from the heart. And with their eyes, they were able to see all that and all the atrocities-- not to let them forget. The whole world shouldn't forget. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Glucksman.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, you have many members of your family here today. Can you tell us who is here with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on my right is Robert Horowitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is your?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my son-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My granddaughter, Alyson, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gorgeous Alyson. My beautiful daughter, Jeannie. My beautiful wife, Libby. On my left, that's my beautiful grandson, our prince.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, after what you've experienced in your life and what we had a chance to talk about today, what words-- how would you describe what it means to you today to have-- I know this isn't your whole family, but to have your family here with you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's-- it's our life. That's what we are living for-- our family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Jeannie, when you think about your life and growing up, how much of this experience are you aware of, in terms of what your father went through?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father especially, and my mother, have always been very open-- have always spoken about what they've gone through, especially my father. Actually, he loves a crowd, loves an audience. And as soon as there are a few of us together, no matter what the occasion is, no matter what the-- the reason, whoever is over-- ultimately the conversation will always end up, at some point, talking about the Holocaust and what it has meant to him. And everybody that we have ever been in contact with has always been interested to listen. You know, people just want to hear more and more, and my father has been very open and very willing and very into talking about it. So we all know about it. We're all aware of it. That has made them that much more special. People just are in awe of what they've gone through, and what has made them very special parents. As-- I mean, any sibling would love their parents, but there's just so much more to be impressed with that they have gone through so much. Endured the hardships, and yet they're here. And it has given them maybe that much more zest for living. They appreciate what they have. They-- their whole world, as my father and mother have said, is their children and grandchildren, and they can't do enough for them. And we all love them and appreciate them irregardless of what they've gone through, but just that much more. And-- and it's just very important that everybody know that this is something that did happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it meant to you in your life and how you raise your children? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We're just-- I think because my parents have gone through so much, they've instilled warmth. They're very warm people. And I think, and hope, that we have that happen in our family. We're just very warm, close, open-minded. And I don't know if it's because of their experiences, but we're just very warm together-- very family-oriented people. I know Seth has just finished a course on the Holocaust. And my dad has spoken at Alyson's school, and will hopefully, get to speak at Seth's school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Seth, what is your-- well, in this case your grandfather, but your grandparents' experience, what does it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their experience means a lot to me. Just knowing about it, knowing about his experiences firsthand and all of the stories that he has told my family and myself have been real important because I mean, I feel that the only good that can be taken away from an experience that he went through is that people can understand that something like this can happen to humanity. And I feel that much closer to it just because I have a relative that went through it. And it's been-- it's given me a lot more of a stronger Jewish identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just that I value being Jewish that much more. Because it's so much of a-- it's-- it's very special to have-- to be, like, the roots of somebody who went through the Holocaust, just to be the offspring of-- of a survivor. There's not that many out there and really fortunate to have that. To have my-- my grandfather survive that, and also feel very fortunate to be a witness to all of his stories. And just, it's a lot easier to pass it on to for everyone else to learn about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thanks. Alyson, how about for you? What has it meant to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my grandpa has told me a lot of stories about it. And I don't really like to hear about it, because it upsets me a lot to think that he went through all that, but I do like learning about it. And it has an effect on me because I feel so bad that he went through that and that anyone went through that. And it, like-- I don't know-- it makes me sad when I think about it, so I don't really try to. But it's scary that it could, like, happen again or something and that scares me, but-- I don't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what's it been like for you, in terms of this experience, marrying into the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's the values that Sidney has passed along to-- to Jeannie and myself and to our grandchildren is, I think, the thing we take the most.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what those values be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Appreciate life, sense of fairness, will to-- to survive, strong family bonds, a real zest for-- for life, and just a warmth in the way that you-- you treat people and learn from the negative experience he had to go through. And I'm very appreciative of the fact that if he didn't survive through all this, I wouldn't have-- wouldn't have this family, this wonderful family sitting here. Very appreciative for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Glucksman, I know we'll be interviewing you shortly, but what has your life with your husband meant to you in sharing this experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we've been very happy together, and we always talk about it, always. I have different stories. He got his stories. And we always share, even we have friends and they coming up for dinner. And any time we talk about something else, and we wind up talking about the Holocaust. This one word here, and hear it all-- all over again-- the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Glucksman, before we conclude, is there any last message you'd like to share with your family or anything before we end? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like to enjoy them for many years. [INTERPOSING VOICES] [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Sounds good, huh. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Be to their weddings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be around when they get married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Mr. Glucksman. Go ahead. I'm sorry, you were going to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just-- just to enjoy them.

speaker: interviewee
text: To enjoy the kids, to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We enjoy them all the time, anyway. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks for sharing your story, Mr. Glucksman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of, Mr Glucksman?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Regina or Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This had to be taken like, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would say probably about '47, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's me, and this was taken in '47, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is Jerome Klein. This is the soldier that brought me-- liberated me in Dachau and brought me over to this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about when?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his office.

speaker: interviewer
text: About when was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is me in a UNRRA truck. UNRRA is the organization that supplied food to the displaced persons camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bad Reichenhall. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From left to right, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's me, this is-- the second is a Jewish policeman from the displaced persons camps in Bad Reichenhall. And the guy on the right, that's a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Siegel, I think. I don't recall his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in '46, 1946. This is my embarkation card when I arrived to New York on the ship, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is our wedding picture in New York City. The guy on the left standing up was a very close friend of mine. We went to school together. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to-- I used to work for him when I arrived here in New Haven.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my best man at our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is the woman seated on the right-- on the far right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the far right, the first one is the wife of my best man.

speaker: interviewer
text: And next to her? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't see that part. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anyone else there that's--

speaker: interviewee
text: I can see Libby's-- my wife's grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, is that the woman to her left?

speaker: interviewee
text: To her left.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her aunt, her uncle. The one on the left-- another uncle's wife with her daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and the date of your wedding?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date of my wedding is '49. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the left is Mrs. Klein, that's Jerome's mother. That's the lady that brought me over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jerome, again, was who?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Jerome is her son.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Jerome was who to you? Is-- he's the person who liberated you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the middle baby, that's my daughter, Jeannie. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on my left, that's my beautiful wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Libby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Libby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken with Jeannie-- was two months before Jeannie was born.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before, that she was there already.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- Jeannie's birthday is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] April '40.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK, anything else about this picture, Mr. Glucksman?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much, Mr. Glucksman.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
